text: Following diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon positions a Nathanson's retractor to elevate the liver, providing better access to the surgical field.
The Nathanson's retractor is positioned to retract the left lobe of the liver, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The video shows a large hiatal hernia with the stomach visibly protruding into the chest cavity, creating a noticeable gap at the hiatus.
A 5mm port is inserted at the level of the anterior axillary line using a lateral approach.
The Nathanson's retractor is positioned just below the costal margin on the left side, retracting the left lobe of the liver to provide a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a 5mm instrument to grasp the stomach, employing two hand instruments to perform the passed flaccida approach, manipulating the stomach within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut tissue, likely in the area of the large hiatal hernia where the stomach is protruding into the chest.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the past flaccida and the peritoneum over the right crus, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to take small bites and cut the peritoneum over the right crus, carefully grasping the tissue with the instrument.
The surgeon achieves hemostasis using surgical instruments, ensuring control of bleeding in the operative field.

The surgeon asks the assistant to apply gentle traction, revealing the peritoneal lining over the right crus. A small incision is made with a harmonic scalpel to enter the plane while preserving the lining.
The surgeon maintains the peritoneum over the right crus while dissecting laterally into the white fascia structures using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon carefully dissects near the right pleura using a harmonic scalpel and a left-hand instrument, ensuring not to injure or create a rent in the pleura.
The posterior vagus nerve is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The assistant provides traction, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the surgical area.

The surgeon observes the lateral attachments on the left side of the patient, identifying the peritoneum over these structures. The peritoneum is grasped and the underlying structures are pushed down, revealing the peritoneal lining over the left cusp.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue on the left side, ensuring the pleura remains intact during dissection.
The surgeon uses the left hand instrument to grasp the peritoneal lining and cuts it with a harmonic scalpel, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon maintains the peritoneal lining over the left crus, ensuring the tissue remains intact during the procedure.
The anterior vagus nerve is visible in the surgical field, positioned near the peritoneal lining over the left crust.
The surgeon pushes down the anterior vagus nerve and enters the plane above it, using instruments to manipulate the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses instruments to separate the esophagus from surrounding tissues, maintaining the lining over the left crus.
The surgeon manipulates the lower esophagus anteriorly, maintaining its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the anterior vagus nerve, ensuring it remains intact and uninjured during the procedure.
Small vessels are visible in the surgical field as the surgeon navigates around the anterior vagus during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon uses an armory scalpel to cut small vessels in the area, aiming to prevent bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue around the lower esophagus, focusing on the posterior aspect, while ensuring careful handling to avoid injury to surrounding structures.
The assistant grasps the OG junction and lifts it upward, providing better exposure for the surgical field.
The posterior vagus nerve and esophagus are visible, with the area below prepared for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon uses a left-hand instrument to push and dissect the surrounding structures, creating a posterior window.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon gently performs dissection using a harmonic scalpel to minimize bleeding, carefully cutting small vessels in the area.
The posterior vagus nerve is visible above the esophagus, clearly identifiable in the surgical field.
The surgeon places umbilical tape around the dissected structures, likely for retraction or identification purposes.
The surgeon uses umbilical tape to apply traction around the esophagogastric (OG) junction, enhancing maneuverability for further dissection.
The assistant holds the umbilical tape in place, providing traction around the OG junction to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection trans-hiatally, using the harmonic scalpel to carefully navigate and separate tissues in the area.
The posterior vagus nerve is clearly visible in the surgical field, positioned above the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon lifts the posterior vagus nerve, dissecting and separating it from the underlying aorta using careful manipulation.
The surgeon exposes the aorta posteriorly, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to identify anatomical structures while minimizing bleeding in the surgical area.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect tissue, revealing the aorta posteriorly. The dissection is carried out around the left crus.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneal reflection, revealing the shiny, smooth surface of the peritoneum.
The surgeon pushes the peritoneal reflection to create a plane between the esophagus and the peritoneal reflection, facilitating further dissection.
The surgeon performs a careful dissection, taking small bites of tissue to avoid bleeding while separating anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs dissection in the area around the left crus, using harmonic devices to carefully separate tissue layers, with the aorta visible posteriorly.
The surgeon carefully maintains the peritoneum over the left crus, ensuring it remains intact during the dissection process.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, carefully separating tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully detach the lower esophagus from its pleural attachments, ensuring precise separation of these structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects the anatomical structures, proceeding slowly to avoid injury to surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects between the pericardium and surrounding tissues, using a harmonic scalpel to carefully manage and separate small fibers.
The intraabdominal esophagus is visible with sufficient length achieved, but some posterior attachments remain and need to be cut.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the fibers and complete the dissection from the posterior side, ensuring all attachments are severed.
The surgeon identifies and dissects flimsy attachments, noting the presence of small vessels within the surgical field.
The surgeon applies traction to demonstrate the extent of esophagus mobilization, revealing the length of the intraabdominal esophagus achieved during the dissection.
The surgeon has mobilized a significant length of the esophagus, with visible separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a measuring tool to assess the length of the mobilized esophagus, ensuring adequate mobilization for the procedure.
The surgeon uses traction to measure the mobilized esophagus, which appears to be approximately six to seven centimeters in length.
The surgeon uses an umbilical tape marked at one-centimeter intervals to measure the mobilized esophagus.

The surgeon measures the mobilized esophagus, noting six centimeters of mobilization with traction, as indicated by the markings on the umbilical tape. The assistant provides traction during the measurement.
The surgeon measures the intra-abdominal esophagus, noting that it extends approximately three centimeters below the crura.
The surgeon completes the dissection, indicating that the mobilized tissue length is sufficient for the procedure.
The surgeon addresses the short gastric vessels, preparing to manage them during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon begins dissecting the short gastric vessels at the lower end of the spleen, along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon uses a ligature device, likely to secure or ligate tissue structures during the hernia repair procedure.
The assistant holds the cut end while the surgeon uses a ligature to cut along the greater curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon uses a ligature to detach the attachments along the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring the blood supply is maintained.
The surgeon addresses the short gastric vessels near the spleen, ensuring careful handling to maintain the blood supply along the greater curvature.
The surgeon encounters small adhesions in the surgical area, which may require careful dissection to separate the tissues.
The surgeon carefully addresses the adhesions in the surgical field, using instruments to separate and manage the tissue connections.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing both the crura and the upper border of the pancreas, indicating the endpoint of the procedure.
The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the fundus wrap being passed through the posterior window, with clear visibility of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs the shoeshine maneuver, a technique used to ensure proper positioning and tension of the mesh during hernia repair.
The surgeon gently manipulates the tissue, allowing it to separate easily, indicating minimal resistance or adhesions in the area.
The surgeon demonstrates the floppy Nissen fundoplication, showing that the wrap is secure and not retracting.
The surgeon begins the crural approximation, bringing the crura together to close the defect in the diaphragm.
The surgeon uses 2-0 sutures to secure the fundus wrap, visible through the posterior window created during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully performs suturing, ensuring the needle's entry and exit points are visible to avoid injuring the underlying aorta and caudate lobe of the liver, which could cause bleeding and obscure the surgical field.
The surgeon places a figure-of-eight suture on the crura, securing the tissue with precise needle entry and exit points.
The surgeon places three throws in the suture to secure it firmly in place during the crural approximation.
The surgeon secures the figure-of-eight suture on the crura with three throws, ensuring the suture remains in place without requiring additional assistance.
The surgeon prepares to place an additional stitch in the surgical field, ensuring the suture holds securely in place.
The surgeon performs three throws of sutures to secure the tissue, ensuring the approximation holds without additional assistance.
The surgeon completes coral suturing, securing the tissue layers together with a series of stitches.
The surgeon checks the coronal approximation of the sutures to ensure they are not too tight, observing the tension and alignment of the repaired tissue.
The surgeon attempts to wrap the lower esophagus, while the assistant holds the umbilical tape in place.
The surgeon observes that there is still some traction on the stomach, indicating tension in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach, preparing to address these connections during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon frees a portion of the stomach from adhesions, visibly releasing it from traction.
The surgeon addresses the adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach, carefully separating these structures to relieve traction on the stomach.
The assistant provides traction while the surgeon uses a ligature to manipulate the tissue in the area with adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon releases adhesions between the pancreas and the posterior wall of the stomach, carefully separating the connected tissues.
The surgeon visually identifies adhesions in the surgical field, likely involving the hernia repair site.
The surgeon ensures the posterior aspect of the stomach is completely free, preparing for a floppy mission by releasing any remaining adhesions.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field to ensure there is no bleeding after releasing adhesions during the hernia repair procedure.
Dense adhesions are visible in the surgical field, requiring careful dissection to separate them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to cut through the dense adhesions, separating them from surrounding tissues.
The splenic artery becomes visible in the surgical field, indicating its proximity to the area being operated on.
The surgeon carefully separates dense adhesions to free the splenic artery, using a ligature to cut through the fibrous tissue.
The surgeon visually confirms the splenic artery is intact and well-maintained, with no signs of injury, amidst the surrounding tissue.
The assistant holds traction around the esophagogastric junction, preventing it from retracting back into the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a 360-degree flap around the junction, carefully manipulating the tissue to maintain the integrity of surrounding structures.
The surgeon places sutures laterally around the surgical site, ensuring wide coverage to secure the repair.
The surgeon performs a 360-degree wrap around the junction, securing the tissue with sutures to reinforce the repair site.
The surgeon creates a floppy Nissen fundoplication by placing a second stitch from the stomach to the esophagus and back to the stomach, securing the wrap just above the esophagogastric junction.
The surgeon places the third suture at the level of the esophagogastric (OG) junction, securing the wrap in position.
The surgeon secures the wrap to the right crura, preventing its migration into the chest cavity.
The surgeon secures the wrap to the right crura at the specified level, ensuring stability and preventing migration.
The surgeon places the second suture posterior to the wrap, securing it to both crura, ensuring the wrap remains stable.
The surgeon secures the wrap at two locations, ensuring it remains stable and properly positioned during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon carefully removes the Nathanson's retractor from the surgical field under direct visualization.
The surgical field is observed with no visible bleeding present.
